Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The events likely correspond to three distinct patients: patient age 45 male (M) (private insurance), patient age 62 female (F) (public insurance), patient age 28 male (M) (private insurance). They have distinct sequences.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The grouping should be consistent: each patient follows a flow: Registration -> Triage (if in ER) -> Imaging etc. For the surgery patient, registration in Surgery department, then doctor consultation, surgery preparation, surgery performed, recovery room, then discharge. For the 45-year-old male: registration in ER, initial triage, X-ray imaging, doctor consultation (diagnostic), prescription, discharge. For the 62-year-old female: registration in ER, initial triage, lab test requested, lab test completed, doctor consultation, prescription, discharge.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Thus three cases: case A (45M), case B (62F), case C (28M).

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: case 1 includes events: 1,4,7,12,15. Case 2: events 2,5,8,10,11,14,17. Case 3: events 3,6,9,13,16,18.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Check if any other events belong? All events are covered. Check the sequence for case 2: Register (2) -> Triage (5) -> Lab test requested (8) -> Lab test completed (10) -> Doctor consultation (11) -> Prescription (14) -> Discharge (17). That makes sense. Case 1: Register (1) -> Triage (4) -> X-ray imaging (7) -> Doctor consultation (12) -> Discharge (15). Good. Case 3: Register (3) -> Doctor consultation (6) -> Surgery prep (9) -> Surgery performed (13) -> Recovery (16) -> Discharge (18). Good.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: List all events again: 1. Register patient, age 45 M, ER 2. Register patient, age 62 F, ER 3. Register patient, age 28 M, Surgery ... Thus we see three distinct flows, with no overlapping ages/genders.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: We could output grouping. One can show as: Case 1 (Patient: 45-year-old male, private insurance): - 1 (Register) ... The user might want a table.
